evals.prompt: We have a microservices checkout flow: Client calls OrderService, which synchronously calls PaymentService (Stripe) and then asynchronously publishes to an InventoryQueue consumed by InventoryService. Draw a sequence diagram showing the full checkout flow including what happens when PaymentService returns a card-declined error.
evals.expected_output: A Mermaid sequence diagram that: uses sync arrows for Order->Payment, async arrows for the queue, models the card-declined failure path with an alt block, shows the InventoryQueue as an explicit participant, and optionally shows a compensation path
assertions: The diagram uses asynchronous arrow syntax (->) or (-) for the InventoryQueue communication rather than synchronous arrows
The InventoryQueue is modeled as an explicit participant in the diagram (not a direct line between OrderService and InventoryService)
An alt or opt block is present to model the payment failure (card-declined) path
The output is a valid .mmd or Mermaid code block with sequenceDiagram type
The skill ran the lint_diagram.js validation script before presenting the output

evals.prompt: I'm building a SaaS analytics platform. It has: a React frontend, a GraphQL API Gateway, an Auth service (Auth0), a TimeSeries DB (InfluxDB), a Relational DB (Postgres), a Redis cache, a Kafka event bus, an ETL pipeline service, a Data Warehouse (Snowflake), an ML Inference service, a Notification service (email + push), a Billing service (Stripe), and external data source webhooks. Generate me a C4 context diagram to show the landscape.
evals.expected_output: Either a properly scoped C4 Context diagram (Person + System + External System actors only, no internal implementation details) OR the assistant should proactively split into L1 context + multiple zoomed-in L2 component views because >20 nodes would make a single diagram unreadable
assertions: The skill loads the c4-diagrams.md reference file before generating the diagram
The diagram does NOT attempt to render all 13+ internal components in a single flat diagram (god diagram anti-pattern avoided)
If a single diagram is produced, it stays at C4 Context level (users, systems, external systems) rather than mixing implementation details
The output includes at least one Mermaid code block
Bi-directional arrows (<-->) are not used in the diagram

evals.prompt: Design a database schema for a multi-tenant blogging platform. Each tenant has users, and users can write posts. Posts have tags (many-to-many). Comments are threaded (replies to comments). Users can follow other users. Generate an ERD.
evals.expected_output: A Mermaid ERD with correct entity relationships: tenants, users (with tenant_id FK), posts (with author FK), tags, post_tags join table, comments with self-referential parent_id, user_follows join table. Should use proper ERD relationship syntax.
assertions: The skill loads the erd-diagrams.md reference file
The ERD includes an entity for tenants with a foreign key relationship to users
The many-to-many post/tags relationship is resolved via a join table (post_tags or similar)
Threaded comments are modeled with a self-referential relationship (parent_id or similar)
The user-follows relationship is modeled as a join table or self-referential association
The output uses Mermaid erDiagram syntax
